explanations.48: This tells bash to stop immediately if any command fails, instead of continuing—it's a safety feature for scripts.
explanations.49: This tells bash to error out if you try to use a variable that was never defined, catching typos.
explanations.52: This creates an array with two characters: a filled block (█) for the completed part and a lighter block (▒) for the incomplete part of the progress bar.
explanations.74: This uses printf with a special format to print empty spaces equal to the terminal width, which clears the line—the '>&2' sends this to error output instead of regular output.
explanations.88: This declares the variable as read-only (immutable after set) and uses the '?' operator to make it a required parameter that throws an error if missing.
explanations.103: This calculates what percentage of the total work is done using integer division (no decimals).
explanations.111: This subtracts the width of the text segments from the available terminal width to figure out how much space is left for the bar itself.
explanations.119: This replaces every space character in the progress_segment with the 'completed' character (█), essentially filling in the done portion of the bar.
explanations.155: This sets up a signal handler: when the terminal window is resized (WINCH signal), bash automatically calls the function to recalculate the bar size.
explanations.159: This uses a regex to look for the pattern 'Progress=<number>' in each line, and if found, stores the number in BASH_REMATCH[1].
explanations.167: This trick tests if the script was sourced (included) or run directly—if it was sourced, the return command succeeds; if run directly, it fails.
